turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 22-888, Rudisill versus McDonough.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Since the Second World War, Congress has honored the sacrifices of men and women who've served in the military by enacting GI Bills to provide educational benefits to qualifying service members. Typically, a service member who qualifies under one GI Bill can get 36 months of educational benefits and if a service member earns educational benefits separately under more than one GI Bill, the benefits are capped at an aggregate total of 48 months. This case addresses two overlapping GI Bills and the extent to which a service member who has earned separate entitlements can use them to fund his education. The Montgomery GI Bill provides 36 months of basic educational benefits to qualifying service members, who serve between the years of 1985 and 2030. But after 9/11, in recognition of the particularly arduous nature of military service, Congress passed the post-9/11 GI Bill, which provides enhanced educational benefits to service members who entered service on or after September 11, 2001. Petitioner James Rudisill first enlisted in the United States Army in the year 2000. He served for a total of eight years over three separate periods. His first period of service entitled him to Montgomery benefits and he used some of those benefits to earn his undergraduate degree. Then through Rudisill’s subsequent periods of service, he earned post-9/11 benefits. Rudisill was admitted to Divinity School, where he hoped to earn a degree that he could use to become a chaplain in the Army. He tried to access his post-9/11 benefits to fund that degree, but the VA told him that a provision in the statute, 38 U.S.C. Section 3327(d)(2) limited his post-9/11 benefits to the length of his unused Montgomery benefits. In other words, he was told that he could only get 36 months of education benefits in total, not the 48 months that he thought he had earned. Ultimately, the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit sitting en banc agreed with the VA and held that Rudisill’s benefits were so limited. We now reverse. Today, we hold that service members who, through separate periods of service, accrue educational benefits under both the Montgomery and the post-9/11 GI Bills may use either benefit in any order up to Section 3695’s 48-month aggregate benefits cap. First, we recognize that Rudisill has earned two separate entitlements through his lengthy military service. Absent some other limitation, the statute creates a baseline rule that the VA is obligated to pay him those benefits, and nothing in the statute limits his benefits in the way the government proposes. Section 3322, which says how certain service members can "coordinate" their benefits does not limit Rudisill’s entitlement. Rudisill is not required to coordinate his Montgomery and post-9/11 benefits. Quite simply, a service member who has earned two benefits separately and just wants to use each of the two benefits is not coordinating anything within the meaning of the statute. Finally, Section 3327 which creates a mechanism for service members to swap out their Montgomery benefits for post-9/11 benefits, does not restrict Rudisill’s ability to use his entitlements, making a Section 3327 election is optional, and Rudisill does not forfeit any benefits by declining to make an election under that provision. For these reasons, and others explained in the Court’s opinion, we reverse the judgment below. Justice Kavanaugh has filed a concurring opinion in which Justice Barrett joins. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Alito joins.
